7ab:["$","p","blogv4_4316_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4316_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["BLUE OCEAN STRATEGY"]}],"Creating unique market opportunities"]}]
7ac:["$","p","blogv4_4317_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["A Blue Ocean Strategy is one where the key to success Lies not in competing directly with rivals within a market, but in creating an entirely new market where there are currently no competitors and where the potential for high returns is vast."]}]
7ad:["$","p","blogv4_4318_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Developed by W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne, Blue Ocean Strategy involves a change in strategic thinking towards a mindset that challenges existing market boundaries, rewrites the rules of competition, and creates a new, as yet uncontested, market space. The theory outlines two attitudes to competition: Red Oceans and Blue Oceans."]}]
7ae:["$","p","blogv4_4319_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The current marketplace for all products and services is made up of Red Oceans (bloody battlegrounds), where boundaries are clearly defined and companies operate within the boundaries of their accepted Red Ocean markets. Here, the entrenched battleground is one where companies compete to gain extra market share within the current market boundary."]}]
7af:["$","p","blogv4_4320_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["A very different attitude pervades the Blue Oceans. These are areas of deep, uncharted, almost limitless potential where the aim is not to compete on traditional grounds but to develop products and services that create entirely new markets. In essence, it is creating customers that do not yet exist."]}]
7b0:["$","p","blogv4_4321_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["At its core, Blue Ocean Strategy believes that it is better to create tomorrow’s customers through developing a new market rather than scrabbling around trying to capture existing customers in the current marketplace. There may be many justifications for this approach but, quite simply, the reason seems straightforward: to create a monopoly situation and reap the high rewards before competitors enter the new market."]}]
7b1:["$","p","blogv4_4322_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4322_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Value creation"]}]]}]
7b2:["$","p","blogv4_4323_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Value is achieved by integrating the utility of the product with its cost and price. It is not a case of choosing between competing through managing costs or product differentiation: it is about pursuing both. It is this that creates the value that appeals across customer groups, drawing them into a new market. Think of this as maximizing the gap between the utility of the product and its price (facilitated by lower costs) – the larger this gap, the higher the value and the more it attracts customers."]}]

7c3:["$","p","blogv4_4343_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Benchmarking is a continual process that needs to adapt quickly to changes – it is no use measuring activities that are no longer relevant or failing to measure activities that are now more significant. To do this effectively, as well as assessing internal operations, you need a keen awareness of your customers, competitors and companies in other sectors. This ensures that benchmarking is focused on the issues that matter now rather than reflecting the past, and is not blinkered by a narrow, internal focus that risks delivering more of the same."]}]

7c6:["$","p","blogv4_4346_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["From development and launch, through its peak to eventual decline, a product’s life cycle determines the strategy needed to optimize its return at each stage and to develop further products to ensure ongoing profit-ability and competitiveness."]}]
7c7:["$","p","blogv4_4347_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Although not an exact science, the duration of each stage varies according to the product and the markets involved. Some life cycles are obviously shorter than others – such as technology products. With very short life cycles, it is essential to maximize returns as quickly as possible and to be continually developing the next products. A long-lasting branded product, despite undergoing many life cycles, enjoys continuity from its brand name. Companies, however, still have to manage the life cycles of such branded products – planning the next improvement and managing the replacement of the current version."]}]

7c9:["$","ol","blogv4_4349_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-decimal space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4349_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4349_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Development"]}]," – this includes entirely new products and changes or improvements to existing products"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4350_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4350_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Introduction"]}]," – at this stage, costs can be high relative to revenue"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4351_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4351_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Growth"]}]," – revenue rises and offsets costs"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4352_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4352_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Maturity"]}]," – growth slows and competition rises"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4353_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4353_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Decline"]}]," – sales decline due to increased competition or changing customer preferences"]}]]}]
7ca:["$","p","blogv4_4354_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4354_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["The following describes tactics appropriate to each stage:"]}]]}]
7cb:["$","p","blogv4_4355_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4355_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Development"]}],"Development can be very costly, with unexpected delays, so cash-flow issues are paramount. Researching what customers are looking for and testing prototypes with potential customers will help you develop the right products with fewer glitches – as well as promoting a ready-made pool of customers. Importantly, product development is an ongoing process, ensuring that new products or improvements to existing products are ready to replace current products."]}]
7cc:["$","p","blogv4_4356_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4356_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Introduction"]}],"Getting the launch right is essential. Raising product awareness quickly requires promotional and advertising investment – depending on the nature of the product, targeting early adopters can be useful at this stage. An aggressive pricing strategy can achieve fast market penetration – although this will depend on the brand’s attributes. You could also consider minimizing distribution costs by limiting the availability of the product."]}]
7cd:["$","p","blogv4_4357_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4357_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Growth"]}],"In the face of more competition, but still with considerable potential revenue and falling unit costs, strategy needs to focus on outcompeting rivals, delivering extra value to customers and increasing market share. Further promotional offers, marketing and advertising campaigns, attractive prices and promoting the product’s brand will strengthen your position."]}]
7ce:["$","p","blogv4_4358_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4358_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Maturity"]}],"Given the influx of competitors, a company is faced with several strategic options to strengthen its market share, including: product differentiation, entering new markets, attracting rivals’ customers, a price war, and reducing costs to maintain competitive pricing and profitability. It is important at this stage to monitor the financial situation and the viability of the different options."]}]
7cf:["$","p","blogv4_4359_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4359_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Decline"]}],"With falling sales and reduced margins, any plans and further investment should be considered carefully. Reducing the available options for the product and reducing the number of markets the product is offered in will re-duce costs. Catering to your core customers to cement their loyalty can also boost profits at this stage. Other tactics to extend the life of a product include product extensions and entering previously untapped markets."]}]
7d0:["$","p","blogv4_4360_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4360_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["WEEK 30"]}]]}]
7d1:["$","p","blogv4_4361_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4361_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["SYSTEMS THINKING"]}],"Building better companies"]}]
7d2:["$","p","blogv4_4362_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["A company is a collection of systems, and systems within systems. These all need to operate individually and collectively, to drive the business forward. A company’s systems need to work with strategy, and they need to be open, adaptive and understood."]}]
7d3:["$","p","blogv4_4363_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Traditional approaches to strategy have emphasized the mechanics of how things work. This can result in too much complexity and ‘over-engineering’, with processes and systems being overly focused on the present, unable to adapt and failing to win people over. The fundamental flaw is setting a predetermined solution at the start of any redesign, which then influences subsequent thinking, narrowing views and ambitions, and misses better options. Often, the result of re-engineering is an expensive disappointment."]}]
7d4:["$","p","blogv4_4364_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["In","The Fifth Discipline",", Peter Senge revolutionized business re-engineering by arguing that solutions should be considered only after fully understanding the relationships within and between systems (including the behaviours involved) and examining all related problems and issues. Essentially: go back to basics, look deeper and search further, before you start thinking about solutions. Such open systems thinking builds teams, promotes creativity and develops new approaches. It works with the company’s long-term strategy, enabling adaptability and continual improvement. It is not the easiest approach: it is time-consuming and mentally demanding and generates an overwhelming number of questions. It works best when the right culture and mindset exist."]}]
7d5:["$","p","blogv4_4365_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4365_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["There are seven steps to successful systems thinking:"]}]]}]
7d6:["$","ol","blogv4_4366_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-decimal space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4366_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Explore the situationGather the information you need without making judgments or looking for causes and effects. At this stage, do two things: Cast your net wide, collating as much information as possible. Be objective and detached (see things as they are, without an agenda)."}],["$","li","blogv4_4367_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4367_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Describe the system"]}],"To understand what you are dealing with, list and describe the things that have happened – including the culture, people and atmosphere. Identify, date and examine trends and patterns. Position each factor on a diagram to show the relationships that exist between them. This highlights how aspects work together and reveals negative and positive feedback loops to enable you to analyze the systems in more depth later."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4368_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4368_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Build models"]}],"Mathematical and IT tools are useful but they will take you only so far because systems need to be considered as they really function if they are to be understood and improved."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4369_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4369_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Compare your model to what is actually happening"]}],"Check your model against reality to see whether it fits and whether you have understood it correctly or have missed something."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4370_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4370_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Identify potential improvements"]}],"Once you have confirmed that your model is an accurate representation of what is happening, explore ways in which the system can be improved."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4371_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4371_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Implement your improvements"]}],"Monitor changes and identify any further improvements that could be made. It is essential to win people over – successful change depends on people’s willingness to work positively with the new systems."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4372_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4372_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Repeat the process"]}],"Systems thinking is a continuous activity; companies need to adapt to change and to take advantage of new opportunities."]}]]}]
7d7:["$","p","blogv4_4373_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4373_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["WEEK 31"]}]]}]

A key aspect of awareness of market barriers is that they increase our focus on external issues. In short, it forces us to look up and see the business horizon in much greater detail."]}]
7da:["$","p","blogv4_4376_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4376_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Overview"]}]]}]
7db:["$","p","blogv4_4377_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The word ‘barrier’ is slightly misleading. 7e1:["$","ul","blogv4_4383_b",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","0",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"There are many barriers to entry, including:"}],["$","li","1",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"the high cost of capital"}],["$","li","2",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"other companies owning patents and proprietary technology"}],["$","li","3",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"high research and development costs of developing necessary products"}],["$","li","4",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"expensive technology"}],["$","li","5",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"existing companies enjoying economies of scale that you can’t afford to match"}],["$","li","6",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"a restricted number of government licences"}],["$","li","7",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"the expense of (or lack of access to) effective distribution channels"}],["$","li","8",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Your product not being different enough from market leaders."}]]}]

7e5:["$","p","blogv4_4387_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["For example, the ideal scenario for an established company is to have high entry barriers and low exit barriers. Because of this, some of the best-laid plans can go awry or fail to achieve their potential because of simple oversights or by a failure to properly explore an issue. 7f5:["$","p","blogv4_4403_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Porter’s Generic Competitive Strategies describe how a company develops competitive advantage across its chosen market. There are three generic strategies: cost leadership, differentiation and focus."]}]
7f6:["$","p","blogv4_4404_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4404_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Overview"]}]]}]
7f7:["$","p","blogv4_4405_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["A company chooses to pursue one of two types of competitive advantage: either with lower costs than its competitors, or by differentiating itself along dimensions valued by customers so it can command a higher price. A company also chooses one of two types of scope: either focus (offering its products to selected segments of the market) or industry-wide, offering its product across many market segments. The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980."]}]
7f8:["$","p","blogv4_4406_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4406_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Cost leadership"]}]]}]
7f9:["$","p","blogv4_4407_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The strategic aim is to offer competitive prices by reducing costs and to also use lower costs to raise profit margins, fund discount campaigns, or launch an aggressive price war to gain market share and eliminate the competition. Reducing costs can also open up new markets that were less able to sustain higher prices. Another advantage of lowering costs is providing flexibility should suppliers raise prices unexpectedly and suddenly, without you also having to raise prices."]}]
7fa:["$","p","blogv4_4408_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The risks, however, are that other companies can copy your methods, eroding any advantage you have, and the lack of investment in research and development will leave your products looking dated and inefficient compared to those of competitors with better equipment and methods."]}]
7fb:["$","p","blogv4_4409_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4409_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Differentiation"]}]]}]
7fc:["$","p","blogv4_4410_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Developing distinctive products for different segments separates you from the competition. It creates product desirability, strengthens your brand, promotes customer loyalty, provides competitive advantage, enables higher prices and delivers higher returns. Your products can be differentiated from those of your competitors but you can also differentiate your own products from one another to target different customer groups and markets."]}]
7fd:["$","p","blogv4_4411_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The risks are higher costs and waste and the potential for more complex operations."]}]
7fe:["$","p","blogv4_4412_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4412_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Focus"]}]]}]
7ff:["$","p","blogv4_4413_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["While focus incorporates aspects of cost leadership and differentiation, it is concerned with targeting products and services at one market segment, gaining increased share in that segment. The risk is that this will produce a narrow view that is overly focused on the short term, on too few factors, and on a less lucrative or unstable market and thus fails to see potential elsewhere."]}]

802:["$","p","blogv4_4416_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Using PESTLIED analysis improves awareness of the impact of external factors. 803:["$","p","blogv4_4417_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4417_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Overview"]}]]}]
804:["$","p","blogv4_4418_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["When running a business it is always advisable to keep a wide range of external matters in view. PESTLIED provides a format to check that strategy and plans have adequately accounted for external factors and to conduct an overall review of how the company is performing and how it could be improved. Significantly, by valuing and using this format, it encourages people to always look beyond the company to notice opportunities and threats. It therefore works well with the technique of SWOT analysis."]}]

810:["$","p","blogv4_4430_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4430_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Overview"]}]]}]
811:["$","p","blogv4_4431_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["When things need to change, people often prefer manageable adjustments because they are cautious and dislike uncertainty. While some issues can be solved with smaller improvements, sometimes a larger shift in thinking is needed. Having the courage and creativity to change a company’s fundamental business model radically isn’t easy but may be the only real answer to a problem or even point the way to a better future. After all, your current situation is ultimately resting on the paradigm that has got you to this point. So, tweaking this and that further up the line may help to a degree but may not be tackling the root cause of the problem: a flawed or outdated business model. 830:["$","p","blogv4_4465_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["•",["$","strong","blogv4_4465_1",{"className":"font-bold","children":["The interaction between resources."]}]," Resources can combine in a cycle to accelerate their growth. For example, rising sales volumes may lead to more cash and more internal capacity, both of which can be used to generate increasing sales, perhaps by entering new markets, in a self-sustaining cycle. Similarly, product quality (an intangible resource) may lead to increased sales, and this in turn can generate sufficient cash to continue improving product quality (and continue increasing sales). In the same way that resources can interact to reinforce one another, they can also interact by limiting one another."]}]
831:["$","p","blogv4_4466_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["•",["$","strong","blogv4_4466_1",{"className":"font-bold","children":["The fragility of the resource."]}]," Cash, quality, customers, staff, reputation and most other resources can all disappear with remarkable speed and ease. It is, therefore, important to control the main factors likely to damage or undermine resources. For example: cash needs to be monitored and controlled; quality can be eroded by suppliers; service can be undermined by the attitudes of personnel; and brand reputation may be damaged by the actions of distributors."]}]

846:["$","p","blogv4_4491_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Competing firms emphasize and trade off different things that customers value. For example:"]}]
847:["$","ul","blogv4_4492_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4492_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"The UK retailer The Body Shop traded the slick packaging, clinical approach and glamorous image traditionally favored by the cosmetics industry in return for a lower price and a more sustainable identity (see diagram). In the USA South-West Airlines pioneered low-cost aviation by trading the features of traditional air travel in return for the benefits of cheap, point-to-point travel. Multiplex cinemas traded the conventional convenience and centrality of town centre locations in return for the benefits of space and a different experience for customers. Home Depot expanded into out-of-town locations on freeways and employed ex-contractors as a way of providing a new level of service and value for customers who did not typically visit home building stores."}]]}]
848:["$","p","blogv4_4493_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-2xl font-bold leading-relaxed break-words","children":["The concept of value curves highlights several points about market positioning:"]}]
849:["$","ul","blogv4_4494_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4494_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Competing firms emphasize and trade off different values (e.g. luxury may be traded for a lower price). Customers value specific features (e.g. price, packaging) differently at different times. Different values enable firms to target new, different – and possibly un-fulfilled – market segments, potentially increasing the size of the market. Initially, strategic innovators (e.g. South-West Airlines) create new ‘market space’, gradually redefining the market. It can be extremely difficult, if not impossible, for incumbents to successfully copy new arrivals. This is because internal cultural and resource issues keep firms anchored in their conventional way of working. When reviewing a value curve, consider the trend: how are things changing?WEEK 40"}]]}]

84d:["$","ol","blogv4_4498_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-decimal space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4498_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["New entrants"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4499_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Substitute products"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4500_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Buyers"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4501_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Suppliers"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4502_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Existing competitors."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4503_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4503_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["New entrants"]}],"Ask yourself how easy it is for new companies to enter the market. There are many factors to consider, including barriers to entry (such as patents and high set-up costs), attractiveness of profit margins and the strength of your brand."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4504_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4504_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Substitutes"]}],"Assess how easy it is for your products to be substituted by other products. This includes all alternatives – not just similar products. For example, airlines compete with train and coach companies, not just other airlines."]}],["$","li","blogv4_4505_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4505_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Buyers"]}],"Review how strong your buyers are. Is it a buyers’ market? Can buyers switch to competitors easily? Are some of your customers in such a strong position that this leaves you vulnerable? If your business-to-business buyers are operating at low profit margins, what impact will this have on your company?"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4506_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4506_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Suppliers"]}],"Assess the strength of your suppliers. Are you dependent on a particular supplier – and how can this be mitigated? Does the supplier rely on your custom or could it easily take its operating capacity to other companies or sell directly to your customers? Could you use alternative products or methods to reduce your vulnerability?"]}],["$","li","blogv4_4507_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Existing competitorsUnderstand your competitors and how you compare to them. What threat do they pose? What are their strengths and weaknesses? Could there be a price war or other aggressive strategies – and would you be able to survive such tactics? Are they innovative? Are customers able to move to other companies easily? Now many competitors are there? Which companies are the strongest? Are there any newcomers ready to take the market by storm or render your products redundant?"}]]}]

85a:["$","ul","blogv4_4527_b-parent",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","blogv4_4527_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Build a team that has a mix of strengths and approaches. Define the design challenge – to do this, understand your market, customers, technology and constraints and use this information to develop key themes. Visit experts, and gather information on markets, customers – and ideas generally. Share ideas. Brainstorm and vote – this involves intensive brainstorming and discussion to imagine new concepts and ideas based around the main themes. Develop a fast prototype. Test and refine the prototype, streamlining ideas to improve the proto-type and to overcome obstacles – at this stage, evaluate and prioritize ideas and decide how they can be implemented. Focus on the prototype and produce a final solution. Give credit to those involved – this promotes motivation and encourages continued innovative thinking.WEEK 43"}]]}]

85c:["$","p","blogv4_4529_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Edward de Bono sees creativity as a learnable skill, one that is best harnessed through formal techniques. 85d:["$","p","blogv4_4530_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Formal creativity works because it works with the way everyone’s brains work: both consciously and subconsciously, we automatically filter, categorize, process and organize information. Building on this, de Bono argues that parallel thinking is more effective for generating the results that make a difference to companies. (Parallel thinking is when each individual puts forward their own thoughts in parallel with those of others. In this way, each individual is able to complement, enrich and build on one another’s thinking, rather than competing or attacking the thoughts of others.)"]}]

85f:["$","p","blogv4_4532_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4532_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Understanding creativity"]}]]}]
860:["$","p","blogv4_4533_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Creativity solves problems, challenges existing methods, and provides a better and constantly improving way forward. Given the reward, companies need to know how best to harness creativity in a way that is useful. A major flaw in traditional brainstorming is that it assumes that, if you give people the freedom to express themselves, they will magically become creative. This is not the case. For organizations, useful creativity needs to be a formal activity that requires thinking that provokes and challenges a current situation and then searches for answers."]}]
861:["$","p","blogv4_4534_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":[["$","strong","blogv4_4534_0",{"className":"font-bold","children":["Provoke, challenge and search for solutions"]}]]}]
862:["$","p","blogv4_4535_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Given the brain’s natural inclination to organize information and think laterally, we can tackle issues by simply taking a random starting point. Our brains will automatically process information, make connections and point us in new directions. Allowing such randomness in selecting a starting point is important. It suggests new possibilities and takes thinking along new paths. Significantly, it is likely that our brains have already processed information and are subconsciously suggesting such opening gambits because they could be highly relevant. This serves to break us out of the current doldrums and set us on a new course."]}]
863:["$","p","blogv4_4536_b",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"mb-4 text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":["Next, our new thinking needs to move forward: to challenge the information it is processing. Just because something has always been done a certain way does not mean it is carved in stone: methods can always be improved upon. Constantly questioning and challenging is a mindset that is a huge source of competitive advantage precisely because it is the way that companies create value from their resources. An important point to remember is that even when something seems to be working and is successful it doesn’t mean it is the best that it can be. Once thinking challenges the norm, we will automatically explore alternative and potentially better solutions."]}]

876:["$","ul","blogv4_4560_b",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","0",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"When innovating, how effectively do you:"}],["$","li","1",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"engage as many people as possible …?"}],["$","li","2",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"… and build an open, diverse and positive team?"}],["$","li","3",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"define the specific challenge or issue?"}],["$","li","4",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"challenge assumptions: yours and other people’s?"}],["$","li","5",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"confront challenges and problems?"}],["$","li","6",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"understand that good ideas can come from anywhere?"}],["$","li","7",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"follow through – by being practical and realistic, and planning implementation?"}],["$","li","8",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"focus on the benefits as well as the potential pitfalls?"}],["$","li","9",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"question? 8ab:["$","ul","blogv4_4622_b",{"className":"mb-4 ml-7 list-disc space-y-2","children":[["$","li","0",{"style":{"color":"#1f2937"},"className":"text-lg leading-relaxed break-words","children":"Money. b6d:["$","p","fullv4_1794_b",{"children":["WEEK 20"]}]
b6e:["$","p","fullv4_1795_b",{"children":["THE 7S MODEL"]}]
b6f:["$","p","fullv4_1796_b",{"children":["Assessing business performance"]}]
b70:["$","p","fullv4_1797_b",{"children":["The 7S model is a framework for assessing the performance of a company. It views all seven elements as equally important because they impact on each other â€“ with failure in one area undermining the others. By appreciating how they are related, and assessing performance from this perspective, companies and teams can better align activities to achieve goals."]}]
b71:["$","p","fullv4_1798_b",{"children":["Overview"]}]
b72:["$","p","fullv4_1799_b",{"children":["First developed in the 1970s by McKinsey and refined by Tom Peters, Robert Waterman and Richard Pascale, the 7S model works from the principle that success relies on simultaneously pursuing a combination of seven hard and soft aspects of running a business. Known for changing peopleâ€™s thinking at the time, it still provides a useful framework for assessing and improving a company or how a team is working â€“ identifying gaps and enabling adjustments to be made to ensure that all seven aspects are aligned, working together, and supporting and reinforcing one another. By knowing how things are interrelated, the framework raises awareness of the full impact of any changes."]}]
b73:["$","ol","fullv4_1800_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_1800_b",{"children":["StrategyThese are plans that determine, define and outline how to fulfill the companyâ€™s goals and purpose and to achieve competitive advantage."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1801_b",{"children":["StructureThis is how the company is organized and how each part relates to the others."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1802_b",{"children":["SystemsThis is about how both formal and informal business processes function."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1803_b",{"children":["Shared values (superordinate goals)These are the companyâ€™s beliefs, values and guiding mission that draw people together and that directly influence their approach, thinking and actions."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1804_b",{"children":["SkillsThese are the capabilities of both the people and the organization."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1805_b",{"children":["StaffThis concerns the nature, type and general abilities of the people employed."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1806_b",{"children":["StyleThis is the organizationâ€™s culture and style of leadership that, along with having an internal impact, determine how people outside the organization view the company."]}]]}]
b74:["$","p","fullv4_1807_b",{"children":["The main point is that all seven elements are interrelated, with each affecting the others. In this, it can be viewed as an early proponent of holistic business. Significantly â€“ and this is of particular relevance to leaders today â€“ it reveals how underperformance can be attributed to neglect in any one of the seven aspects, regardless of strong focus and capabilities in one or more of the others. Richard Pascale subsequently argued that, while it is generally important to view all seven as equally significant to achieving success, having shared values (superordinate goals) is the element that binds all the others together."]}]
b75:["$","p","fullv4_1808_b",{"children":["WEEK 21"]}]
b76:["$","p","fullv4_1809_b",{"children":["THE RULE OF 150"]}]
b77:["$","p","fullv4_1810_b",{"children":["A bold way to create the right working conditions"]}]
b78:["$","p","fullv4_1811_b",{"children":["This rule is about limiting the number of people at any one location to 150."]}]
b79:["$","p","fullv4_1812_b",{"children":["Overview"]}]
b7a:["$","p","fullv4_1813_b",{"children":["The rule is based on the idea that 150 is the largest group size that people can deal with â€“ beyond that number, it is increasingly difficult to form bonds with others. If groups are larger, hierarchies, regulations and formal measures are required. However, with fewer than 150, goals can be achieved informally and people work better and are happier, more motivated and more productive."]}]
b7b:["$","p","fullv4_1814_b",{"children":["Why it works"]}]
b7c:["$","p","fullv4_1815_b",{"children":["Co-workers find socializing, teamworking, innovating, collaborating and sharing knowledge easier to achieve in groups of fewer than 150 people. By organizing operations into smaller groups, large companies can gain the benefit of smaller groups â€“ being closer, driven, entrepreneurial, supportive and productive."]}]
b7d:["$","p","fullv4_1816_b",{"children":["The rule in practice"]}]
b7e:["$","p","fullv4_1817_b",{"children":["Gore Associates, a high-tech firm, uses this rule. It has 15 plants all within 20 kilometres (12 miles) of one another, and each with fewer than 150 employees. It has resisted the option of merging its separate sites â€“ despite potential cost savings â€“ because the small size of each unit ensures that everyone knows everyone else and works well together."]}]
b7f:["$","p","fullv4_1818_b",{"children":["By organizing itself in this way, Gore, despite being a large company with thousands of employees, is still able to enjoy the entrepreneurial approach of a small start-up. Each unit enjoys the benefits of collective management, which are:"]}]
b80:["$","p","fullv4_1819_b",{"children":["â€¢ improved communicationâ€¢ greater initiativeâ€¢ flexibility"]}]
b81:["$","p","fullv4_1820_b",{"children":["It is notable that employee turnover is significantly less than the industry average and the company has enjoyed sustained profitability and growth for over 35 years."]}]
b82:["$","p","fullv4_1821_b",{"children":["This does not mean that Gore has no control or input. It has put a strong managerial system in place to oversee each unit, to ensure that activities are coordinated and efficient. The company also encourages a sense of community and teamwork within these groups â€“ after all, the rule only means that it is possible for workers to form positive bonds with each other, so efforts must still be made to ensure that this happens. In addition, Gore makes sure that it develops a sense of community across the company by encouraging people to communicate and collaborate with workers from other groups."]}]
b83:["$","p","fullv4_1822_b",{"children":["WEEK 22"]}]
b84:["$","p","fullv4_1823_b",{"children":["THE SERVICE PROFIT CHAIN"]}]
b85:["$","p","fullv4_1824_b",{"children":["Managing the vital link between people and profit"]}]
b86:["$","p","fullv4_1825_b",{"children":["The service profit chain highlights how employee engagement drives improvements in company performance. When employees are able to see the impact of their actions, it changes their approach and improves results."]}]
b87:["$","p","fullv4_1826_b",{"children":["The idea"]}]
b88:["$","p","fullv4_1827_b",{"children":["The service profit chain is based on the premise that market leadership requires an emphasis on managing value drivers â€” those factors that have the greatest impact on success and provide the most benefit to customers. This concept is then focused on the value drivers that are the most important determinants of success: employee retention, employee satisfaction and employee productivity â€” it is these that strongly influence customer loyalty, revenue growth and profitability."]}]
b89:["$","p","fullv4_1828_b",{"children":["How the service profit chain works"]}]
b8a:["$","p","fullv4_1829_b",{"children":["In practice: Sears"]}]
b8b:["$","p","fullv4_1830_b",{"children":["In the 1990s US-based retailer Sears reversed significant losses by focusing on employee issues in order to turn around the companyâ€™s fortunes. They examined:"]}]
b8c:["$","p","fullv4_1831_b",{"children":["â€¢ how employees felt about working at the companyâ€¢ how employee behaviour affected customersâ€¢ how customersâ€™ experience affected profits"]}]
b8d:["$","p","fullv4_1832_b",{"children":["Sears asked employees to estimate how much profit was made for each dollar sold. The average answer was 46 cents while the real answer was 1 cent â€” demonstrating that profitability was poorly understood. The company introduced changes in order to engage with employees and to get them to understand what influences profitability â€” in particular, to make clear the link between employee behaviour, customer satisfaction and company success. By understanding the implications of their actions, it changed their approach, resulting in sustained improvements in profitability."]}]
b8e:["$","p","fullv4_1833_b",{"children":["In practice: B&Q"]}]
b8f:["$","p","fullv4_1834_b",{"children":["At UK retailer B&Q, each percentage increase in staff turnover was costing the company £1 million. By reducing staff turnover from 35 to 28 per cent through its Employee Engagement Programme, the company reduced costs and increased turnover per employee by 20 per cent."]}]
b90:["$","p","fullv4_1835_b",{"children":["WEEK 23"]}]
b91:["$","p","fullv4_1836_b",{"children":["UNDERSTANDING AND AVOIDING INERTIA"]}]
b92:["$","p","fullv4_1837_b",{"children":["When success traps us in the past"]}]
b93:["$","p","fullv4_1838_b",{"children":["It might seem counterintuitive to warn people about the dangers of success but that is exactly what Donald Sull did when he developed the concept of â€˜active inertiaâ€™ â€” where people repeat the strategies and activities that have worked well in the past."]}]
b94:["$","p","fullv4_1839_b",{"children":["A reliance on previous thinking and approaches â€” the formula of success â€” can cause a company to fail to respond properly to new developments. By applying past approaches to new conditions, the end result can be a downward spiral â€” leaving an organization vulnerable to more dynamic companies with approaches better suited to the new environment."]}]
b95:["$","p","fullv4_1840_b",{"children":["How active inertia works"]}]
b96:["$","p","fullv4_1841_b",{"children":["A firm correctly discerns gradual shifts and developments in the external environment, but fails to respond effectively."]}]
b97:["$","p","fullv4_1842_b",{"children":["Managers get trapped by success, often responding to the most disruptive changes by accelerating activities that succeeded in the past."]}]
b98:["$","p","fullv4_1843_b",{"children":["The source of active inertia is a companyâ€™s success formula, the unique set of strategic frames, resources, processes, relationships and values that collectively influence managersâ€™ actions."]}]
b99:["$","p","fullv4_1844_b",{"children":["With time and repetition, people stop considering alternatives to their formula. The individual components of the success formula grow less flexible."]}]
b9a:["$","p","fullv4_1845_b",{"children":["How active inertia happens"]}]
b9b:["$","p","fullv4_1846_b",{"children":["Active inertia occurs because people come to rely on a past formula of success, where accepted approaches become entrenched and people stop considering alternatives. Consequently, people continue to respond to external changes by pursuing fixes and activities that worked in the past. However, these responses are likely to be ineffectual because they are based on past success and not current and future needs."]}]
b9c:["$","p","fullv4_1847_b",{"children":["Why a past success formula does not guarantee a successful future"]}]
b9d:["$","p","fullv4_1848_b",{"children":["Essentially, like it or not, our brains are lazy â€” subconsciously preferring the easy route to solving problems and then, equally subconsciously, superimposing a solid layer of reasons to justify our decisions. So it is hardly surprising that our brains fool us into being happy to rely on approaches that have proven successful in the past: it is easy and we have a ready-made wall of rock-solid excuses to hand."]}]
b9e:["$","p","fullv4_1849_b",{"children":["As individuals, our thinking, strategies, methods, use of resources, relationships and values all become firmly entrenched. The consequence for companies is that this formula becomes so deeply embedded that they are left vulnerable when faced with changing conditions."]}]
b9f:["$","p","fullv4_1850_b",{"children":["It is understandable that past approaches should be so revered and relied upon â€” they are, after all, the reason for the companyâ€™s current success. However, we should keep in mind that this formula is exactly that: suited to the current, stable situation â€” not the future. Companies can suddenly find themselves commercially stranded."]}]
ba0:["$","p","fullv4_1851_b",{"children":["The bottom line is that, when faced with new developments, your approach needs to change accordingly â€” essentially, the survival of the fittest depends on adaptation."]}]

bc3:["$","p","fullv4_1886_b",{"children":["WEEK 26"]}]
bc4:["$","p","fullv4_1887_b",{"children":["THE PARETO PRINCIPLE"]}]
bc5:["$","p","fullv4_1888_b",{"children":["Finding the right locus and answer using the 80:20 rule"]}]
bc6:["$","p","fullv4_1889_b",{"children":["Pareto analysis arose from Vilfredo Paretoâ€™s observation that many activities break down into an 80:20 ratio, where 80 per cent of output is due to 20 per cent of the contributory factors. This observation is now used to focus business strategy, problem-solving and operations on the key inputs that are responsible for 80 per cent of the outcome."]}]
bc7:["$","p","fullv4_1890_b",{"children":["How it works"]}]
bc8:["$","p","fullv4_1891_b",{"children":["The 80:20 ratio applies both to positive and negative situations, providing a useful means of dealing quickly with problems or opportunities. In other words, by identifying the small number of key factors that are contributing most to a situation, we can better focus efforts to achieve the desired result."]}]
bc9:["$","p","fullv4_1892_b",{"children":["Pareto analysis is only as good as the data that is used, so we need to ensure that all contributory factors are identified and that appropriate and revealing parameters and measures are established and interpreted correctly. Although not everything falls neatly into an 80:20 rule, Pareto analysis is still useful for identifying the main causal factors."]}]
bca:["$","p","fullv4_1893_b",{"children":["This simple example shows how the process works:"]}]
bcb:["$","ol","fullv4_1894_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_1894_b",{"children":["Research and discuss the issue, identifying all contributory factors."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1895_b",{"children":["Decide an appropriate time period and method of measurement."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1896_b",{"children":["Measure how frequently each factor occurs (or another measure, such as cost)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1897_b",{"children":["Rank the factors in descending order, with the largest one first."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1898_b",{"children":["Calculate the frequency of each factor as a percentage of the total occurrences (or cost)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1899_b",{"children":["Calculate the cumulative percentage (current percentage plus all previous percentages)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1900_b",{"children":["Depict this information on a graph â€“ with â€˜frequency as a percentage of totalâ€™ as a bar chart and â€˜cumulative percentageâ€™ as a line, adding a third line showing the 80 per cent cut-off point."]}]]}]
bcc:["$","p","fullv4_1901_b",{"children":["All factors that appear to the left of the intersection of the two lines are the ones contributing to 80 per cent of the result â€” these are the factors to focus on."]}]

bce:["$","p","fullv4_1903_b",{"children":["WEEK 27"]}]
bcf:["$","p","fullv4_1904_b",{"children":["BLUE OCEAN STRATEGY"]}]
bd0:["$","p","fullv4_1905_b",{"children":["Creating unique market opportunities"]}]
bd1:["$","p","fullv4_1906_b",{"children":["A Blue Ocean Strategy is one where the key to success lies not in competing directly with rivals within a market, but in creating an entirely new market where there are currently no competitors and where the potential for high returns is vast."]}]
bd2:["$","p","fullv4_1907_b",{"children":["Developed by W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne, Blue Ocean Strategy involves a change in strategic thinking towards a mindset that challenges existing market boundaries, rewrites the rules of competition, and creates a new, as yet uncontested, market space. The theory outlines two attitudes to competition: Red Oceans and Blue Oceans."]}]
bd3:["$","p","fullv4_1908_b",{"children":["The current marketplace for all products and services is made up of Red Oceans (bloody battlegrounds), where boundaries are clearly defined and companies operate within the boundaries of their accepted Red Ocean markets. Here, the entrenched battleground is one where companies compete to gain extra market share within the current market boundary."]}]
bd4:["$","p","fullv4_1909_b",{"children":["A very different attitude pervades the Blue Oceans. These are areas of deep, uncharted, almost limitless potential where the aim is not to compete on traditional grounds but to develop products and services that create entirely new markets. In essence, it is creating customers that do not yet exist."]}]
bd5:["$","p","fullv4_1910_b",{"children":["At its core, Blue Ocean Strategy believes that it is better to create tomorrowâ€™s customers through developing a new market rather than scrabbling around trying to capture existing customers in the current marketplace. There may be many justifications for this approach but, quite simply, the reason seems straightforward: to create a monopoly situation and reap the high rewards before competitors enter the new market."]}]
bd6:["$","p","fullv4_1911_b",{"children":["Value creation"]}]
bd7:["$","p","fullv4_1912_b",{"children":["Value is achieved by integrating the utility of the product with its cost and price. It is not a case of choosing between competing through managing costs or product differentiation: it is about pursuing both. It is this that creates the value that appeals across customer groups, drawing them into a new market. Think of this as maximizing the gap between the utility of the product and its price (facilitated by lower costs) â€” the larger this gap, the higher the value and the more it attracts customers."]}]
bd8:["$","p","fullv4_1913_b",{"children":["Blue Ocean Strategy relies on four main principles:"]}]
bd9:["$","ol","fullv4_1914_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_1914_b",{"children":["Challenging existing market boundaries.Reconstruct the marketplace, identifying and creating new markets and customers. The aim is to bring this demand into existence."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1915_b",{"children":["Keeping focused on the overall picture.Be clear about your goals: what matters and needs to be achieved."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1916_b",{"children":["Minimizing risk.Assess current industry standards and decide what can be:a. eliminated â€” things that are not necessaryb. reduced â€” things that do not need to be done to a high standardc. raised â€” things that should be done betterd. created â€” things that have never been offered before."]}],["$","li","fullv4_1917_b",{"children":["Planning careful implementation.You will need to overcome barriers and secure the resources and the support of your people (especially key influencers)."]}]]}]

c03:["$","p","fullv4_1963_b",{"children":["Traditional approaches to strategy have emphasized the mechanics of how things work. This can result in too much complexity and â€˜over-engineeringâ€™, with processes and systems being overly focused on the present, unable to adapt and failing to win people over. The fundamental flaw is setting a predetermined solution at the start of any redesign, which then influences subsequent thinking, narrowing views and ambitions, and misses better options. Often, the result of re-engineering is an expensive disappointment."]}]
c04:["$","p","fullv4_1964_b",{"children":["In The Fifth Discipline, Peter Senge revolutionized business re-engineering by arguing that solutions should be considered only after fully understanding the relationships within and between systems (including the behaviours involved) and examining all related problems and issues. Essentially: go back to basics, look deeper and search further, before you start thinking about solutions. Such open systems thinking builds teams, promotes creativity and develops new approaches. It works with the companyâ€™s long-term strategy, enabling adaptability and continual improvement. It is not the easiest approach: it is time-consuming and mentally demanding and generates an overwhelming number of questions. It works best when the right culture and mindset exist."]}]
c05:["$","p","fullv4_1965_b",{"children":["There are seven steps to successful systems thinking:"]}]
c06:["$","ol","fullv4_1966_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_1966_b",{"children":["Explore the situationGather the information you need without making judgments or looking for causes and effects. c0b:["$","p","fullv4_1977_b",{"children":["Overview"]}]

c0d:["$","p","fullv4_1979_b",{"children":["The matrix below summarizes the impact of barriers to entry and exit on profitability."]}]

c12:["$","p","fullv4_1984_b",{"children":["Exit barriers"]}]

c14:["$","p","fullv4_1986_b",{"children":["Not only do you need to understand all the costs, legalities and brand issues, you need to understand how barriers work: how they affect you and, importantly, how they will affect your current and potential competitors. c15:["$","p","fullv4_1987_b",{"children":["For example, the ideal scenario for an established company is to have high entry barriers and low exit barriers. c16:["$","p","fullv4_1988_b",{"children":["A much less favourable scenario is having low entry barriers but high exit barriers. c2a:["$","p","fullv4_2008_b",{"children":["Overview"]}]
c2b:["$","p","fullv4_2009_b",{"children":["A company chooses to pursue one of two types of competitive advantage: either with lower costs than its competitors, or by differentiating itself along dimensions valued by customers so it can command a higher price. A company also chooses one of two types of scope: either focus (offering its products to selected segments of the market) or industry-wide, offering its product across many market segments. The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980."]}]
c2c:["$","p","fullv4_2010_b",{"children":["Cost leadership"]}]
c2d:["$","p","fullv4_2011_b",{"children":["The strategic aim is to offer competitive prices by reducing costs and to also use lower costs to raise profit margins, fund discount campaigns, or launch an aggressive price war to gain market share and eliminate the competition. Reducing costs can also open up new markets that were less able to sustain higher prices. Another advantage of lowering costs is providing flexibility should suppliers raise prices unexpectedly and suddenly, without you also having to raise prices."]}]
c2e:["$","p","fullv4_2012_b",{"children":["The risks, however, are that other companies can copy your methods, eroding any advantage you have, and the lack of investment in research and development will leave your products looking dated and inefficient compared to those of competitors with better equipment and methods."]}]

Your products can be differentiated from those of your competitors but you can also differentiate your own products from one another to target different customer groups and markets."]}]

c50:["$","p","fullv4_2046_b",{"children":["Overview"]}]
c51:["$","p","fullv4_2047_b",{"children":["When things need to change, people often prefer manageable adjustments because they are cautious and dislike uncertainty. c69:["$","p","fullv4_2074_b",{"children":["â€˜Resourcesâ€™ is an overused term in business but any factor providing value or benefit, from whatever origin, is a resource that can be used to benefit the business. c6a:["$","p","fullv4_2075_b",{"children":["Managing resources"]}]
c6b:["$","p","fullv4_2076_b",{"children":["Assessing which resources are important involves taking a view across the whole of the business and identifying those factors, direct or indirect, tangible or intangible, that can be expanded and used for competitive advantage. Understanding which resources are most important and how they should be managed requires a clear understanding of the nature of each resource, in terms of the following:"]}]
c6c:["$","p","fullv4_2077_b",{"children":["â€¢ The interaction between resources. Resources can combine in a cycle to accelerate their growth. For example, rising sales volumes may lead to more cash and more internal capacity, both of which can be used to generate increasing sales, perhaps by entering new markets, in a self-sustaining cycle. Similarly, product quality (an intangible resource) may lead to increased sales, and this in turn can generate sufficient cash to continue improving product quality (and continue increasing sales). In the same way that resources can interact to reinforce one another, they can also interact by limiting one another."]}]
c6d:["$","p","fullv4_2078_b",{"children":["â€¢ The fragility of the resource. Cash, quality, customers, staff, reputation and most other resources can all disappear with remarkable speed and ease. It is, therefore, important to control the main factors likely to damage or undermine resources. For example: cash needs to be monitored and controlled; quality can be eroded by suppliers; service can be undermined by the attitudes of personnel; and brand reputation may be damaged by the actions of distributors."]}]
c6e:["$","p","fullv4_2079_b",{"children":["â€¢ The quality of resources. It is worth considering how the quality of resources can be developed. For example, a customer base is a valuable resource, but its quality might be improved by increasing customer loyalty to your brand â€“ for instance, by using customer loyalty schemes."]}]
c6f:["$","p","fullv4_2080_b",{"children":["How resources affect performance"]}]
c70:["$","p","fullv4_2081_b",{"children":["Resources have a special characteristic: they fill and drain over time. To build strong business performance, we need to know:"]}]
c71:["$","p","fullv4_2082_b",{"children":["â€¢ how many resources are availableâ€¢ how fast these numbers are changingâ€¢ how strongly these factors are being influenced by things under our control and by other forcesâ€¢ how resources interrelate with one another"]}]
c72:["$","p","fullv4_2083_b",{"children":["In a system where resources are integrated and working together, what matters is not the uniqueness of individual resources but how they combine and work together to deliver value for customers. c7b:["$","p","fullv4_2092_b",{"children":["â€¢ Indirect resources are those factors that have a bearing on the quality, strength and value of resources. For example, effective training and development policies are an indirect resource, as they build the effectiveness of staff expertise."]}]

c80:["$","p","fullv4_2097_b",{"children":["â€¢ Be clear which of these factors genuinely â€˜accumulateâ€™ through time and which are simply current features of the business. â€˜Qualityâ€™ and â€˜serviceâ€™ reflect the balance between what has to be done and what is available to do it, in which case they do not accumulate. c92:["$","p","fullv4_2119_b",{"children":["1. New entrants"]}]
c93:["$","p","fullv4_2120_b",{"children":["Ask yourself how easy it is for new companies to enter the market. There are many factors to consider, including barriers to entry (such as patents and high set-up costs), attractiveness of profit margins and the strength of your brand."]}]
c94:["$","p","fullv4_2121_b",{"children":["2. Substitutes"]}]
c95:["$","p","fullv4_2122_b",{"children":["Assess how easy it is for your products to be substituted by other products. This includes all alternatives â€“ not just similar products. For example, airlines compete with train and coach companies, not just other airlines."]}]
c96:["$","p","fullv4_2123_b",{"children":["3. Buyers"]}]
c97:["$","p","fullv4_2124_b",{"children":["Review how strong your buyers are. Is it a buyersâ€™ market? Can buyers switch to competitors easily? If your business-to-business buyers are operating at low profit margins, what impact will this have on your company?"]}]
c98:["$","p","fullv4_2125_b",{"children":["4. Suppliers"]}]
c99:["$","p","fullv4_2126_b",{"children":["Assess the strength of your suppliers. Are you dependent on a particular supplier â€“ and how can this be mitigated? Could you use alternative products or methods to reduce your vulnerability?"]}]
c9a:["$","p","fullv4_2127_b",{"children":["5. Existing competitors"]}]

c9d:["$","p","fullv4_2130_b",{"children":["Assessing competitiveness through all five forces will help you to determine how the company is performing, its strengths and weaknesses and the direction it is heading in. Because a weakness of Porterâ€™s approach is the focus on external issues, it is often used alongside complementary models that are better at revealing the internal issues that impact on a companyâ€™s competitiveness."]}]

1142:["$","p","fullv4_4163_b",{"children":["This approach sounds simple but there are several key points to note to ensure a successful strategy:"]}]
14a0:T840,â€¢ Ensure that your strategy creates a unique strategic position. 1170:["$","p","fullv4_4210_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4210_0",{"children":["THE 7S MODEL"]}]]}]
1171:["$","p","fullv4_4211_b",{"children":["Assessing business performance"]}]
1172:["$","p","fullv4_4212_b",{"children":["The 7S model is a framework for assessing the performance of a company. It views all seven elements as equally important because they impact on each other â€“ with failure in one area undermining the others. By appreciating how they are related, and assessing performance from this perspective, companies and teams can better align activities to achieve goals."]}]
1173:["$","p","fullv4_4213_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4213_0",{"children":["Overview"]}]]}]
1174:["$","p","fullv4_4214_b",{"children":["First developed in the 1970s by McKinsey and refined by Tom Peters, Robert Waterman and Richard Pascale, the 75 model works from the principle that success relies on simultaneously pursuing a combination of seven hard and soft aspects of running a business. Known for changing peopleâ€™s thinking at the time, it still provides a useful framework for assessing and improving a company or how a team is working â€“ identifying gaps and enabling adjustments to be made to ensure that all seven aspects are aligned, working together, and supporting and reinforcing one another. By knowing how things are interrelated, the framework raises awareness of the full impact of any changes."]}]
1175:["$","p","fullv4_4215_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4215_0",{"children":["1. Strategy"]}],"These are plans that determine, define and outline how to fulfill the companyâ€™s goals and purpose and to achieve competitive advantage."]}]
1176:["$","p","fullv4_4216_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4216_0",{"children":["2. Structure"]}],"This is how the company is organized and how each part relates to the others."]}]
1177:["$","p","fullv4_4217_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4217_0",{"children":["3. Systems"]}],"This is about how both formal and informal business processes function."]}]
1178:["$","p","fullv4_4218_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4218_0",{"children":["4. Shared values (superordirlate goals)"]}],"These are the companyâ€™s beliefs, values and guiding mission that draw people together and that directly influence their approach, thinking and actions."]}]
1179:["$","p","fullv4_4219_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4219_0",{"children":["5. Skills"]}],"These are the capabilities of both the people and the organization."]}]
117a:["$","p","fullv4_4220_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4220_0",{"children":["6. Staff"]}],"This concerns the nature, type and general abilities of the people employed."]}]
117b:["$","p","fullv4_4221_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4221_0",{"children":["7. Style"]}],"This is the organizationâ€™s culture and style of leadership that, along with having an internal impact, determine how people outside the organization view the company."]}]
117c:["$","p","fullv4_4222_b",{"children":["The main point is that all seven elements are interrelated, with each affecting the others. In this, it can be viewed as an early proponent of holistic business. Significantly â€“ and this is of particular relevance to leaders today â€“ it reveals how underperformance can be attributed to neglect in any one of the seven aspects, regardless of strong focus and capabilities in one or more of the others. Richard Pascale subsequently argued that, while it is generally important to view all seven as equally significant to achieving success, having shared values (superordinate goals) is the element that binds all the others together."]}]

1181:["$","p","fullv4_4227_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4227_0",{"children":["Overview"]}]]}]
1182:["$","p","fullv4_4228_b",{"children":["The rule is based on the idea that 150 is the largest group size that people can deal with â€“ beyond that number, it is increasingly difficult to form bonds with others. If groups are larger, hierarchies, regulations and formal measures are required. However, with fewer than 150, goals can be achieved informally and people work better and are happier, more motivated and more productive."]}]
1183:["$","p","fullv4_4229_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4229_0",{"children":["Why it works"]}]]}]
1184:["$","p","fullv4_4230_b",{"children":["Co-workers find socializing, teamworking, innovating, collaborating and sharing knowledge easier to achieve in groups of fewer than 150 people. By organizing operations into smaller groups, large companies can gain the benefit of smaller groups â€“ being closer, driven, entrepreneurial, supportive and productive."]}]
1185:["$","p","fullv4_4231_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4231_0",{"children":["The rule in practice"]}]]}]
1186:["$","p","fullv4_4232_b",{"children":["Gore Associates, a high-tech firm, uses this rule. It has 15 plants all within 20 kilometres (12 miles) of one another, and each with fewer than 150 employees. It has resisted the option of merging its separate sites â€“ despite potential cost savings â€“ because the small size of each unit ensures that everyone knows everyone else and works well together."]}]
1187:["$","p","fullv4_4233_b",{"children":["By organizing itself in this way, Gore, despite being a large company with thousands of employees, is still able to enjoy the entrepreneurial approach of a small start-up. Each unit enjoys the benefits of collective management, which are"]}]

1189:["$","p","fullv4_4235_b",{"children":["It is notable that employee turnover is significantly less than the industry average and the company has enjoyed sustained profitability and growth for over 35 years."]}]
118a:["$","p","fullv4_4236_b",{"children":["This does not mean that Gore has no control or input. 1190:["$","p","fullv4_4242_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4242_0",{"children":["How the service profit chain works"]}]]}]

1197:["$","p","fullv4_4249_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4249_0",{"children":["WEEK 23"]}]]}]
1198:["$","p","fullv4_4250_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4250_0",{"children":["UNDERSTANDING AND AVOIDING INERTIA"]}]]}]
1199:["$","p","fullv4_4251_b",{"children":["When success traps us in the past"]}]
119a:["$","p","fullv4_4252_b",{"children":["It might seem counterintuitive to warn people about the dangers of success but that is exactly what Donald Sull did when he developed the concept of â€˜active inertiaâ€™ â€“ where people repeat the strategies and activities that have worked well in the past."]}]
119b:["$","p","fullv4_4253_b",{"children":["A reliance on previous thinking and approaches â€“ the formula of success â€“ can cause a company to fail to respond properly to new developments. By applying past approaches to new conditions, the end result can be a downward spiral â€“ leaving an organization vulnerable to more dynamic companies with approaches better suited to the new environment."]}]
119c:["$","p","fullv4_4254_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4254_0",{"children":["How active inertia works"]}]]}]
119d:["$","p","fullv4_4255_b",{"children":["A firm correctly discerns gradual shifts and developments in the external environment, but fails to respond effectively."]}]
119e:["$","p","fullv4_4256_b",{"children":["Managers get trapped by success, often responding to the most disruptive changes by accelerating activities that succeeded in the past."]}]
119f:["$","p","fullv4_4257_b",{"children":["The source of active inertia is a companyâ€™s success formula, the unique set of strategic frames, resources, processes, relationships and values that collectively influence managersâ€™ actions."]}]
11a0:["$","p","fullv4_4258_b",{"children":["With time and repetition, people stop considering alternatives to their formula. The individual components of the success formula grow less flexible."]}]
11a1:["$","p","fullv4_4259_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4259_0",{"children":["How active inertia happens"]}]]}]
11a2:["$","p","fullv4_4260_b",{"children":["Active inertia occurs because people come to rely on a past formula of success, where accepted approaches become entrenched and people stop considering alternatives. Consequently, people continue to respond to external changes by pursuing fixes and activities that worked in the past. However, these responses are likely to be ineffectual because they are based on past success and not current and future needs."]}]
11a3:["$","p","fullv4_4261_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4261_0",{"children":["Why a past success formula does not guarantee a successful future"]}]]}]
11a4:["$","p","fullv4_4262_b",{"children":["Essentially, like it or not, our brains are lazy â€“ subconsciously preferring the easy route to solving problems and then, equally subconsciously, superimposing a solid layer of reasons to justify our decisions. So it is hardly surprising that our brains fool us into being happy to rely on approaches that have proven successful in the past: it is easy and we have a ready-made wall of rock-solid excuses to hand."]}]
11a5:["$","p","fullv4_4263_b",{"children":["As individuals, our thinking, strategies, Methods, use of resources, relationships and values all become firmly entrenched. The consequence for companies is that this formula becomes so deeply embedded that they are left vulnerable when faced with changing conditions."]}]
11a6:["$","p","fullv4_4264_b",{"children":["It is understandable that past approaches should be so revered and relied upon â€“ they are, after all, the reason for the companyâ€™s current success. However, we should keep in mind that this formula is exactly that: suited to the current, stable situation â€“ not the future. Companies can suddenly find themselves commercially stranded."]}]
11a7:["$","p","fullv4_4265_b",{"children":["The bottom line is that, when faced with new developments, your approach needs to change accordingly â€“ essentially, the survival of the fittest depends on adaptation.",["$","strong","fullv4_4265_1",{"children":["WEEK 24"]}]]}]
11a8:["$","p","fullv4_4266_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4266_0",{"children":["THE SIX Rs OF BUSINESS"]}]]}]

11ab:["$","p","fullv4_4269_b",{"children":["Having all company activities support one other enables us to develop the right mindset, strategy and approach for growing a successful business. 11ac:["$","p","fullv4_4270_b",{"children":["â€¢ Reasonâ€¢ Revenueâ€¢ Rousersâ€¢ Reputationâ€¢ Relationshipsâ€¢ Resilience."]}]

11ae:["$","p","fullv4_4272_b",{"children":["The Six Rs should work together, supporting one other and never undermining other business activities or goals. As companies can discover to their cost (witness the damage to sales when legal tax avoidance is revealed), any aspect of running a business can have serious consequences. Conversely, when the various corporate activities support one other, they will strengthen the brand and promote success. Essentially, everyone and all activities should pull together. To have parts, even unwittingly, pulling in different directions will derail strategy and cause a company to veer off course."]}]

11bb:["$","p","fullv4_4285_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4285_0",{"children":["WEEK 25"]}]]}]

11bd:["$","p","fullv4_4287_b",{"children":["How to manage your product portfolio"]}]
11be:["$","p","fullv4_4288_b",{"children":["Identifying which products and investments should be continued (and at what level of investment) is a complicated task.
