11bf:["$","p","fullv4_4289_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4289_0",{"children":["How it works"]}]]}]
11c0:["$","p","fullv4_4290_b",{"children":["The model uses a matrix, each box representing a type of product: Star, Cash cow, Question mark and Dog. Products are located in a quadrant according to market growth and market share. The category a product falls into enables you to see whether it is worth pursuing. By Looking at the matrix, it is easy to see why each category has certain characteristics and prospects."]}]
11c1:["$","p","fullv4_4291_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4291_0",{"children":["Star"]}]]}]
11c2:["$","p","fullv4_4292_b",{"children":["Given the high market growth, this product is obviously a rising star and should be pursued. 11c3:["$","p","fullv4_4293_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4293_0",{"children":["Cash cow"]}]]}]
11c4:["$","p","fullv4_4294_b",{"children":["Clearly, given the large market share, there is still a lot of potential for generating revenue. It would be wise to monitor market conditions closely to prevent losses should the market decline rapidly."]}]
11c5:["$","p","fullv4_4295_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4295_0",{"children":["Question mark"]}]]}]
11c6:["$","p","fullv4_4296_b",{"children":["If a product falls into this category, there are issues that need to be addressed before a decision can be made. A key factor in making a decision is having deep-enough pockets either to wait for higher returns as the market grows or to turn it into a Star by securing a stronger market share."]}]
11c7:["$","p","fullv4_4297_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4297_0",{"children":["Dog"]}]]}]

11c9:["$","p","fullv4_4299_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4299_0",{"children":["WEEK 26"]}]]}]
11ca:["$","p","fullv4_4300_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4300_0",{"children":["THE PARETO PRINCIPLE"]}]]}]
11cb:["$","p","fullv4_4301_b",{"children":["Finding the right locus and answer using the 80:20 rule"]}]
11cc:["$","p","fullv4_4302_b",{"children":["Pareto analysis arose from Vilfredo Paretoâ€™s observation that many activities break down into an 80:20 ratio, where 80 per cent of output is due to 20 per cent of the contributory factors. This observation is now used to focus business strategy, problem-solving and operations on the key inputs that are responsible for 80 per cent of the outcome."]}]
11cd:["$","p","fullv4_4303_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4303_0",{"children":["How it works"]}]]}]
11ce:["$","p","fullv4_4304_b",{"children":["The 80:20 ratio applies both to positive and negative situations, providing a useful means of dealing quickly with problems or opportunities. In other words, by identifying the small number of key factors that are contributing most to a situation, we can better focus efforts to achieve the desired result."]}]
11cf:["$","p","fullv4_4305_b",{"children":["Pareto analysis is only as good as the data that is used, so we need to ensure that all contributory factors are identified and that appropriate and revealing parameters and measures are established and interpreted correctly. Although not everything falls neatly into an 80:20 rule, Pareto analysis is still useful for identifying the main causal factors."]}]
11d0:["$","p","fullv4_4306_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4306_0",{"children":["This simple example shows how the process works"]}]]}]
11d1:["$","ol","fullv4_4307_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_4307_b",{"children":["Research and discuss the issue, identifying all contributory factors."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4308_b",{"children":["Decide an appropriate time period and method of measurement."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4309_b",{"children":["Measure how frequently each factor occurs (or another measure, such as cost)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4310_b",{"children":["Rank the factors in descending order, with the largest one first."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4311_b",{"children":["Calculate the frequency of each factor as a percentage of the total occurrences (or cost)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4312_b",{"children":["Calculate the cumulative percentage (current percentage plus all previous percentages)."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4313_b",{"children":["Depict this information on a graph â€“ with â€˜frequency as a percentage of totalâ€™ as a bar chart and â€˜cumulative percentageâ€™ as a line, adding a third line showing the 80 per cent cut-off point."]}]]}]
11d2:["$","p","fullv4_4314_b",{"children":["All factors that appear to the left of the intersection of the two lines are the ones contributing to 80 per cent of the result â€“ these are the factors to focus on."]}]
11d3:["$","p","fullv4_4315_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4315_0",{"children":["Example of how the Pareto Principle can be displayedWEEK 27"]}]]}]
11d4:["$","p","fullv4_4316_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4316_0",{"children":["BLUE OCEAN STRATEGY"]}],"Creating unique market opportunities"]}]
11d5:["$","p","fullv4_4317_b",{"children":["A Blue Ocean Strategy is one where the key to success Lies not in competing directly with rivals within a market, but in creating an entirely new market where there are currently no competitors and where the potential for high returns is vast."]}]
11d6:["$","p","fullv4_4318_b",{"children":["Developed by W. Chan Kim and Renee Mauborgne, Blue Ocean Strategy involves a change in strategic thinking towards a mindset that challenges existing market boundaries, rewrites the rules of competition, and creates a new, as yet uncontested, market space. The theory outlines two attitudes to competition: Red Oceans and Blue Oceans."]}]
11d7:["$","p","fullv4_4319_b",{"children":["The current marketplace for all products and services is made up of Red Oceans (bloody battlegrounds), where boundaries are clearly defined and companies operate within the boundaries of their accepted Red Ocean markets. Here, the entrenched battleground is one where companies compete to gain extra market share within the current market boundary."]}]
11d8:["$","p","fullv4_4320_b",{"children":["A very different attitude pervades the Blue Oceans. These are areas of deep, uncharted, almost limitless potential where the aim is not to compete on traditional grounds but to develop products and services that create entirely new markets. In essence, it is creating customers that do not yet exist."]}]
11d9:["$","p","fullv4_4321_b",{"children":["At its core, Blue Ocean Strategy believes that it is better to create tomorrowâ€™s customers through developing a new market rather than scrabbling around trying to capture existing customers in the current marketplace. There may be many justifications for this approach but, quite simply, the reason seems straightforward: to create a monopoly situation and reap the high rewards before competitors enter the new market."]}]
11da:["$","p","fullv4_4322_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4322_0",{"children":["Value creation"]}]]}]
11db:["$","p","fullv4_4323_b",{"children":["Value is achieved by integrating the utility of the product with its cost and price. It is not a case of choosing between competing through managing costs or product differentiation: it is about pursuing both. It is this that creates the value that appeals across customer groups, drawing them into a new market. Think of this as maximizing the gap between the utility of the product and its price (facilitated by lower costs) â€“ the larger this gap, the higher the value and the more it attracts customers."]}]
11dc:["$","p","fullv4_4324_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4324_0",{"children":["Blue Ocean Strategy relies on four main principles:"]}]]}]
11dd:["$","ol","fullv4_4325_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_4325_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4325_0",{"children":["Challenging existing market boundaries."]}],"Reconstruct the marketplace, identifying and creating new markets and customers. The Blue Ocean is a vast place where demand is unrealized â€“ it doesnâ€™t yet exist. The aim is to bring this demand into existence."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4326_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4326_0",{"children":["Keeping focused on the overall picture."]}],"Be clear about your goals: what matters and needs to be achieved."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4327_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4327_0",{"children":["Minimizing risk."]}],"Assess current industry standards and decide what can be:a. eliminated â€“ things that are not necessaryb. reduced â€“ things that do not need to be done to a high standardc. raised â€“ things that should be done betterd. created â€“ things that have never been offered before."]}],["$","li","fullv4_4328_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4328_0",{"children":["Planning careful implementation."]}],"You will need to overcome barriers and secure the resources and the support of your people (especially key influencers)."]}]]}]
11de:["$","p","fullv4_4329_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4329_0",{"children":["WEEK 28"]}]]}]
11df:["$","p","fullv4_4330_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4330_0",{"children":["BENCHMARKING"]}],"Measuring performance"]}]
11e0:["$","p","fullv4_4331_b",{"children":["Benchmarking establishes standards against which performance can be measured. It is used to assess performance and to set targets across a range of business activities."]}]
11e1:["$","p","fullv4_4332_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4332_0",{"children":["Overview"]}]]}]
11e2:["$","p","fullv4_4333_b",{"children":["The purpose of benchmarking is to improve efficiency and quality, to determine and promote best practice, to maintain competitiveness and to focus people on the need for change and improvement. Carol McNair and Kathleen Leibfried divide benchmarking into four categories as shown below:"]}]
11e3:["$","p","fullv4_4334_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4334_0",{"children":["Category â€” Aim"]}]]}]
11e4:["$","p","fullv4_4335_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4335_0",{"children":["Internal"]}],"Using internal measures to match or surpass current performance, ensure consistent standards throughout the company, eliminate waste and improve operations"]}]
11e5:["$","p","fullv4_4336_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4336_0",{"children":["Competitive"]}],"Using competitorsâ€™ standards to set targets that match or improve upon their performance"]}]
11e6:["$","p","fullv4_4337_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4337_0",{"children":["Industry"]}],"Setting benchmarks that are industry standards"]}]
11e7:["$","p","fullv4_4338_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4338_0",{"children":["Best-in-class"]}],"To match or surpass the standards of the best companies in any industry or country"]}]
11e8:["$","p","fullv4_4339_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4339_0",{"children":["Setting benchmarks"]}]]}]
11e9:["$","p","fullv4_4340_b",{"children":["The data should be free from bias or vested interests. Using an external company to gather evidence and measure standards will help to maintain impartiality."]}]
11ea:["$","p","fullv4_4341_b",{"children":["Successful benchmarking needs everyone to be â€˜on the same pageâ€™ and to understand the process. People need to be clear about what is being measured and what, and it is important to give people the time and resources they need."]}]
11eb:["$","p","fullv4_4342_b",{"children":["While targets need to be realistic and achievable, they also need to ensure that standards are maintained and consistent throughout a company and they should seek to continually improve upon performance. To do this, it is necessary to look at both internal and external evidence."]}]
11ec:["$","p","fullv4_4343_b",{"children":["Benchmarking is a continual process that needs to adapt quickly to changes â€“ it is no use measuring activities that are no longer relevant or failing to measure activities that are now more significant. To do this effectively, as well as assessing internal operations, you need a keen awareness of your customers, competitors and companies in other sectors. This ensures that benchmarking is focused on the issues that matter now rather than reflecting the past, and is not blinkered by a narrow, internal focus that risks delivering more of the same."]}]
11ed:["$","p","fullv4_4344_b",{"children":["By enabling you to know what competitors are doing and what the most innovative, high-performing companies in other industries are achieving, benchmarking will help to maintain your companyâ€™s competitiveness.",["$","strong","fullv4_4344_1",{"children":["WEEK 29"]}]]}]
11ee:["$","p","fullv4_4345_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4345_0",{"children":["THE PRODUCT LIFE CYCLE"]}],"Managing your product portfolio"]}]
11ef:["$","p","fullv4_4346_b",{"children":["From development and launch, through its peak to eventual decline, a productâ€™s life cycle determines the strategy needed to optimize its return at each stage and to develop further products to ensure ongoing profit-ability and competitiveness."]}]
11f0:["$","p","fullv4_4347_b",{"children":["Although not an exact science, the duration of each stage varies according to the product and the markets involved. Some life cycles are obviously shorter than others â€“ such as technology products. With very short life cycles, it is essential to maximize returns as quickly as possible and to be continually developing the next products. A long-lasting branded product, despite undergoing many life cycles, enjoys continuity from its brand name. Companies, however, still have to manage the life cycles of such branded products â€“ planning the next improvement and managing the replacement of the current version."]}]
11f1:["$","p","fullv4_4348_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4348_0",{"children":["There are five stages in the product life cycle:"]}]]}]
11f2:["$","ol","fullv4_4349_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_4349_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4349_0",{"children":["Development"]}]," â€“ this includes entirely new products and changes or improvements to existing products"]}],["$","li","fullv4_4350_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4350_0",{"children":["Introduction"]}]," â€“ at this stage, costs can be high relative to revenue"]}],["$","li","fullv4_4351_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4351_0",{"children":["Growth"]}]," â€“ revenue rises and offsets costs"]}],["$","li","fullv4_4352_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4352_0",{"children":["Maturity"]}]," â€“ growth slows and competition rises"]}],["$","li","fullv4_4353_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4353_0",{"children":["Decline"]}]," â€“ sales decline due to increased competition or changing customer preferences"]}]]}]
11f3:["$","p","fullv4_4354_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4354_0",{"children":["The following describes tactics appropriate to each stage:"]}]]}]
11f4:["$","p","fullv4_4355_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4355_0",{"children":["Development"]}],"Development can be very costly, with unexpected delays, so cash-flow issues are paramount. Researching what customers are looking for and testing prototypes with potential customers will help you develop the right products with fewer glitches â€“ as well as promoting a ready-made pool of customers. Importantly, product development is an ongoing process, ensuring that new products or improvements to existing products are ready to replace current products."]}]
11f5:["$","p","fullv4_4356_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4356_0",{"children":["Introduction"]}],"Getting the launch right is essential. Raising product awareness quickly requires promotional and advertising investment â€“ depending on the nature of the product, targeting early adopters can be useful at this stage. An aggressive pricing strategy can achieve fast market penetration â€“ although this will depend on the brandâ€™s attributes. You could also consider minimizing distribution costs by limiting the availability of the product."]}]
11f6:["$","p","fullv4_4357_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4357_0",{"children":["Growth"]}],"In the face of more competition, but still with considerable potential revenue and falling unit costs, strategy needs to focus on outcompeting rivals, delivering extra value to customers and increasing market share. Further promotional offers, marketing and advertising campaigns, attractive prices and promoting the productâ€™s brand will strengthen your position."]}]
11f7:["$","p","fullv4_4358_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4358_0",{"children":["Maturity"]}],"Given the influx of competitors, a company is faced with several strategic options to strengthen its market share, including: product differentiation, entering new markets, attracting rivalsâ€™ customers, a price war, and reducing costs to maintain competitive pricing and profitability. It is important at this stage to monitor the financial situation and the viability of the different options."]}]
11f8:["$","p","fullv4_4359_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4359_0",{"children":["Decline"]}],"With falling sales and reduced margins, any plans and further investment should be considered carefully. Reducing the available options for the product and reducing the number of markets the product is offered in will re-duce costs. Catering to your core customers to cement their loyalty can also boost profits at this stage. Other tactics to extend the life of a product include product extensions and entering previously untapped markets."]}]

11fa:["$","p","fullv4_4361_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4361_0",{"children":["SYSTEMS THINKING"]}],"Building better companies"]}]
11fb:["$","p","fullv4_4362_b",{"children":["A company is a collection of systems, and systems within systems. These all need to operate individually and collectively, to drive the business forward. A companyâ€™s systems need to work with strategy, and they need to be open, adaptive and understood."]}]
11fc:["$","p","fullv4_4363_b",{"children":["Traditional approaches to strategy have emphasized the mechanics of how things work. This can result in too much complexity and â€˜over-engineeringâ€™, with processes and systems being overly focused on the present, unable to adapt and failing to win people over. The fundamental flaw is setting a predetermined solution at the start of any redesign, which then influences subsequent thinking, narrowing views and ambitions, and misses better options. Often, the result of re-engineering is an expensive disappointment."]}]
11fd:["$","p","fullv4_4364_b",{"children":["In","The Fifth Discipline",", Peter Senge revolutionized business re-engineering by arguing that solutions should be considered only after fully understanding the relationships within and between systems (including the behaviours involved) and examining all related problems and issues. Essentially: go back to basics, look deeper and search further, before you start thinking about solutions. Such open systems thinking builds teams, promotes creativity and develops new approaches. It works with the companyâ€™s long-term strategy, enabling adaptability and continual improvement. It is not the easiest approach: it is time-consuming and mentally demanding and generates an overwhelming number of questions. It works best when the right culture and mindset exist."]}]
11fe:["$","p","fullv4_4365_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4365_0",{"children":["There are seven steps to successful systems thinking:"]}]]}]
11ff:["$","ol","fullv4_4366_b-parent",{"children":[["$","li","fullv4_4366_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4366_0",{"children":["Explore the situation"]}],"Gather the information you need without making judgments or looking for causes and effects. 1220:["$","p","fullv4_4405_b",{"children":["A company chooses to pursue one of two types of competitive advantage: either with lower costs than its competitors, or by differentiating itself along dimensions valued by customers so it can command a higher price. A company also chooses one of two types of scope: either focus (offering its products to selected segments of the market) or industry-wide, offering its product across many market segments. The generic strategy reflects choices made about both the type of competitive advantage and the scope. The concept was first described by Michael Porter in 1980."]}]
1221:["$","p","fullv4_4406_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4406_0",{"children":["Cost leadership"]}]]}]
1222:["$","p","fullv4_4407_b",{"children":["The strategic aim is to offer competitive prices by reducing costs and to also use lower costs to raise profit margins, fund discount campaigns, or launch an aggressive price war to gain market share and eliminate the competition. Another advantage of lowering costs is providing flexibility should suppliers raise prices unexpectedly and suddenly, without you also having to raise prices."]}]

123a:["$","p","fullv4_4431_b",{"children":["When things need to change, people often prefer manageable adjustments because they are cautious and dislike uncertainty. 1256:["$","p","fullv4_4462_b",{"children":["â€˜Resourcesâ€™ is an overused term in business but any factor providing value or benefit, from whatever origin, is a resource that can be used to benefit the business. 1257:["$","p","fullv4_4463_b",{"children":[["$","strong","fullv4_4463_0",{"children":["Managing resources"]}]]}]

1259:["$","p","fullv4_4465_b",{"children":["â€¢",["$","strong","fullv4_4465_1",{"children":["The interaction between resources."]}]," Resources can combine in a cycle to accelerate their growth. For example, rising sales volumes may lead to more cash and more internal capacity, both of which can be used to generate increasing sales, perhaps by entering new markets, in a self-sustaining cycle. Similarly, product quality (an intangible resource) may lead to increased sales, and this in turn can generate sufficient cash to continue improving product quality (and continue increasing sales). In the same way that resources can interact to reinforce one another, they can also interact by limiting one another."]}]
125a:["$","p","fullv4_4466_b",{"children":["â€¢",["$","strong","fullv4_4466_1",{"children":["The fragility of the resource."]}]," Cash, quality, customers, staff, reputation and most other resources can all disappear with remarkable speed and ease. It is, therefore, important to control the main factors likely to damage or undermine resources. For example: cash needs to be monitored and controlled; quality can be eroded by suppliers; service can be undermined by the attitudes of personnel; and brand reputation may be damaged by the actions of distributors."]}]
125b:["$","p","fullv4_4467_b",{"children":["â€¢",["$","strong","fullv4_4467_1",{"children":["The quality of resources."]}]," It is worth considering how the quality of re-sources can be developed. 1268:["$","p","fullv4_4480_b",{"children":["â€¢",["$","strong","fullv4_4480_1",{"children":["Tangible resources"]}]," are those that can be physically seen, such as cash, inventory, sales volumes and customers; typically, these have the highest profile within the organization, as they are the most apparent."]}]
1269:["$","p","fullv4_4481_b",{"children":["â€¢",["$","strong","fullv4_4481_1",{"children":["Intangible resources"]}]," such as service quality, brand reputation or staff expertise are also vitally important to success."]}]
126a:["$","p","fullv4_4482_b",{"children":["Of these, intangible resources can be the hardest to manage (and the easiest to ignore). Several techniques will help ensure that intangible resources are working well with the rest of the business:"]}]
126b:["$","p","fullv4_4483_b",{"children":["â€¢ Identify the most important intangibles.
